main_sections.title: A Homage to Our Brave Soldiers
main_sections.prose_summary:
This section consists of two letters exchanged between friends Soumya (Bengaluru) and Ananda (Chandigarh), centred on the National War Memorial in New Delhi. In her letter, Soumya describes an educational trip to the memorial — a monument spread over 40 acres near India Gate, constructed between April 2018 and February 2019 and inaugurated by the Prime Minister. She explains that the memorial's 29,000 tablets bear the names of over 26,000 fallen soldiers from post-Independence conflicts including the Indo-China conflict of 1962, Indo-Pak Wars of 1965 and 1971, and the Kargil War of 1999. She describes the memorial's four concentric Chakras: the innermost Amar Chakra (Circle of Immortality) with the eternal flame Amar Jawan Jyoti; the Veerta Chakra (Circle of Bravery) with six bronze battle murals; the Tyag Chakra (Circle of Sacrifice) with granite tablets etched with soldiers' names in gold; and the Raksha Chakra (Circle of Protection) ringed by trees representing soldiers guarding the nation. Soumya shares the citations of two Param Vir Chakra recipients — Lance Naik Albert Ekka (1971 war) and, as her presentation focus, Major Padmapani Acharya (Kargil War 1999, MVC), who quoted the Bhagavad Gita before battle and died after capturing his objective. Soumya closes with deep emotion: the visit brought tears but also a resolve to live worthy of the soldiers' sacrifice.

Ananda's reply is equally moving. She reflects that a war memorial is not just a monument but an expression of deep gratitude for the sovereignty soldiers protect. She recalls her own visit to a war memorial in Chandigarh's Bougainvillea Garden (Sector 3), which prompted her to recall the Hindi poem 'Pushp ki Abhilasha' by Makhanlal Chaturvedi — a flower's longing to be cast on the path of soldiers going to sacrifice for the motherland. Ananda mentions Captain Anuj Nayyar (MVC, Kargil War 1999), whose mother wrote the memoir Tiger of Drass. She also shares the National War Memorial mobile app (available in 21 languages), and ends with her plan to create a collage of inspiring lives with her classmates. Both letters together convey themes of patriotism, gratitude, collective memory, and the duty of citizens to honour soldiers.

(II) Work in pairs to complete the table by writing any three ways in which we can celebrate the victories and honour the sacrifices of the soldiers. An example has been done for you (visiting a war memorial). Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

listening.transcript_text: GUNJAN's SPEECH (Armed Forces): Good morning, respected teachers and my dear friends. Today, I, Gunjan, of Grade 7-C, stand before you to share why I dream of joining the Indian Armed Forces when I grow up. Serving our country is the most honourable job one can have. It means protecting and securing our nation, even if it means risking one's life. The thought of wearing the uniform, which can only be earned through hard work, fills me with pride and a strong desire to serve India. The Armed Forces teach you to live a disciplined and organised life. This keeps both the mind and body active. Soldiers are always ready to face challenges because of this discipline. Additionally, regular exercise and training keep soldiers physically fit and ready for any situation. This preparation is something I greatly admire and wish to achieve. Lastly, being a soldier means having new experiences and adventures everyday. Each day brings new challenges, teaching important lessons, and helping one grow. The Armed Forces offer a life filled with honour, self-respect, safety, and security for both the soldier and their families. It is a life that teaches loyalty, duty, service, and honesty. This is why I want to join the Indian Armed Forces. Thank you.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a girl speak about why she wishes to join the Armed Forces when she grows up. As you listen, answer the questions given below in two–three exact words you hear.

Study the words: disregard, inconsequential, immortal, undaunted. These words are made by adding prefixes (dis-, in-, im-, un-) to root words. Match the prefixes with their meanings and root words to create new words. Then complete the paragraph using the new words created.

question_bank.stem: Match prefixes with their meanings and root words to create new words: (re- / badly,wrongly / questionable), (mal- / beyond,outside / activate), (un- / opposed to;against / define), (anti- / former functioning / service), (extra- / lacking;not / ordinary), (ex- / remove / social), (de- / do again). Example: extra- + ordinary = extraordinary.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You have read about the sacrifices made by the Armed Forces during war. However, the Armed Forces also play a key role during peace time. Write a letter to your friend sharing how the Armed Forces contribute their services during the times of natural calamities (floods, earthquakes, etc.), infrastructure development (building bridges in remote areas, etc.) and for welfare initiatives (medical camps, establishment of libraries, etc.). Use the prompts given below.

Every branch of the Armed Forces has a motto. Read the mottos: (1) Indian Army — Sevā Paramo Dharmaḥ (Service Before Self); (2) Indian Air Force — Nabhaḥ Spr̥śaṁ Dīptam (Touch the Sky with Glory); (3) Indian Navy — Sham No Varunah (Be Auspicious Unto Us Oh Varuna). India has many other Forces that serve the nation (e.g. Central Reserve Police Force — CRPF). Find out about such Forces and discuss with the teacher.
Create a collage capturing the lives and stories of people that inspire us. In groups of four, prepare a collage on a chart paper on any such person and present it in the class.

question_bank.stem: In groups of four, create a collage on a chart paper about a person who inspires you and present it in class.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Many women have joined the Armed Forces and are serving the nation. Have a discussion in the class with examples.

main_sections.title: My Dear Soldiers
main_sections.poem_text:
Oh! Defenders of borders
You are great sons of my land
When we are all asleep
You still hold on to your deed
Windy season or snowy days
Or scorching sun's sweltering rays
You are there guarding all the time awake
Treading the lonely expanses as yogis
Climbing the heights or striding the valleys
Defending the deserts or guarding the marshes
Surveillance in seas and by securing the air
Prime of your youth given to the nation!!
Wind chimes of my land vibrate your feat
We pray for you brave men!!
May the Lord bless you all!!
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: 'My Dear Soldiers' by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam is a tribute poem addressed directly to the soldiers of India. Written in second person ('You'), it establishes an intimate, grateful relationship between the nation's citizens ('we') and its protectors ('you'). The poem surveys the breadth of the soldier's sacrifice: across all seasons (windy, snowy, scorching), all terrains (mountains, valleys, deserts, marshes, seas, air), and all hours — including while ordinary citizens sleep. A powerful simile likens the soldier to a yogi, treading lonely expanses with spiritual discipline. The central idea — 'Prime of your youth given to the nation' — captures the magnitude of this sacrifice. The poem closes with a metaphor: wind chimes vibrating to honour the soldiers' feat, followed by a prayer for their blessing. The dominant tone is deeply admiring, respectful, and prayerful. Poetic devices include imagery (terrain and weather descriptions), simile (as yogis), metaphor (wind chimes of my land), alliteration (snowy/season, deserts/defending), and the repeated contrast between 'we' and 'you' to reinforce collective gratitude.

It highlights their untiring commitment and sacrifices as they guard the nation. The soldiers remain vigilant and dedicated, whether it's windy, snowy, or 3. _______________ hot. They walk on 4. _______________ stretches of land like the yogis. Their duty takes them to different terrains, including mountains, valleys, 5. _______________, and marshes. The poet acknowledges that they have given a major part of their 6. _______________ to protect the nation. The poet says the wind chimes show their respect to the soldiers' 7. _______________. The poet concludes with prayers for their well-being and blessings.'

Select the correct option: The tone of the poem is _______. A. Only (v) prayerful B. (i) admiring and (iii) respectful C. (ii) sad, (iii) respectful and (v) prayerful D. (i) admiring, (iii) respectful, (iv) grateful and (v) prayerful

(3) Metaphor because the poet uses wind chimes ___. (4) Repetition of 'you' and 'we' because ___. (5) Alliteration such as 'season or snowy' and ___ to create ___.

listening.transcript_text: POEM: 'A Soldier's Pride' (full text): In fields so vast, our soldiers stand, / With hearts so brave, and firm command, / They guard our land both night and day, / To keep our fears and foes at bay. // With every dawn and setting sun, / Their duty calls, it must be done, / Through stormy skies and blazing heat, / They march along with steady feet. // For love of land, they fight and strive, / To keep our hopes and dreams alive, / With pride and honour, they defend, / Our India, till the very end.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Think and complete the poem titled 'A Soldier's Pride' by filling in the blanks (1. __, 2. __, 3. __, 4. __, 5. __, 6. __, 7. __, 8. __). Then listen to the poem and correct your answers wherever required.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks to complete 'A Soldier's Pride': 'In fields so vast, our soldiers stand, / With hearts so brave, and firm 1. ______________, / They guard our land both night and 2. ______________, / To keep our fears and foes at bay. // With every dawn and setting 3. ______________, / Their duty calls, it must be done, / Through 4. ______________ skies and blazing heat, / They 5. ______________ along with steady feet. // For love of land, they 6. ______________ and strive, / To keep our hopes and 7. ______________ alive, / With pride and honour, they defend, / Our beloved India, till the very 8. ______________.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs and practise reciting the poem 'My Dear Soldiers'. Pause briefly at (/) and emphasise the highlighted words. Count 1-2-3 silently before reciting. Remember: (i) Use volume to add emotion — speak more softly for lines expressing tenderness or solemnity, louder for strength or pride. (ii) Maintain a steady pace; don't rush. (iii) Use appropriate facial expressions. Stand tall with a confident posture to reflect the pride and strength of the soldiers. Then recite aloud in groups of 8–10.
An acrostic poem is one in which the first letter of each line spells out a word, name, or phrase when read vertically. Read the example (SOLDIER). Compose your own acrostic poem.

We cannot thank our soldiers enough. To express your admiration, draft a note of gratitude for their selfless service to the nation. (Word limit: 50 words.) You may begin: 'Dear Bravehearts, I would like to…'

The poem mentions a few terrains (physical features of land) — valleys, deserts, and marshes. Identify the terrains from the box given below. There are two that you do not need. (Box: ghats, river, plateau, delta, bay, peninsula, mountains.)

question_bank.stem: Identify the terrains from the box (ghats, river, plateau, delta, bay, peninsula, mountains) that match physical features of land mentioned or implied in the poem. Two items do not apply.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the phrases from the poem: 'treading the lonely expanses', 'climbing the heights', 'scorching sun'. These are collocations — specific word pairings that frequently appear together. Fill in the blanks by choosing the words given in brackets that collocate appropriately.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with the correct collocating word: (1) The hikers reached the ____________ (summit/peak) of the mountain after several days of trekking through challenging conditions. (2) The ____________ (empty/barren) landscape of the desert made it nearly impossible to find any water sources. (3) In the rainforest, the ____________ (heavy/dense) forest provides a habitat for a diverse range of wildlife. (4) Chitra is a very ____________ (heroic/adventurous) person and enjoys mountain climbing. (5) I'll try to get back in time, but I'm not ____________ (doing/making) any promises.

main_sections.title: Rani Abbakka
main_sections.prose_summary:
Rani Abbakka is a historical narrative drama about Abbakka Chowta, the 16th-century queen of Ullal (on the Karnataka coast), who fiercely resisted Portuguese colonial domination. When the Portuguese demanded tribute from Ullal — a sovereign trading kingdom — Abbakka refused, declaring that Ullal owed nothing to Portugal. Her husband Veera Narsimha thought this rash, but Abbakka rallied the people and soldiers of Ullal behind her cause. She forged an alliance with the Zamorin of Kozhikode and established profitable trade with Arabia, directly defying the Portuguese economic blockade.

The Portuguese, incensed, launched repeated attacks. Each time, Abbakka — leading from the front, sword in hand — drove them back. She organised a midnight counter-attack, entering the Portuguese fort herself, killing their commander, and freeing prisoners. The Portuguese governor, humiliated, escalated military pressure. When treachery came from within (her estranged husband collaborated with the Portuguese), Abbakka's forces were weakened. She continued to resist until she was finally captured and later died in captivity, unbroken. The narrative text (which appears in illustrated/image format in the textbook and therefore not fully captured in PDF text extraction) is followed by detailed comprehension and vocabulary activities. The vocabulary and grammar work on this section covers: vocabulary (word meanings from text), cause-and-effect analysis, spelling rules (i before e, silent letters), expressions with 'laugh', direct-to-indirect speech conversion, and pronunciation of multisyllabic words. Abbakka's story reinforces the chapter's theme of courage, sovereignty, and bravehearted resistance — this time from a pre-Independence woman warrior.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. (I) Solve the riddle and share your answer: 'She was a queen who led the fight, / She fought for her country and her right. / With a shining sword in her hand, / Her son on her back in a cloth band / Remembered as a warrior grand, / She died defending her beloved Motherland. Who is she?' (II) Think of any other women warriors who made a significant contribution to India's struggle for Independence. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Solve the riddle: 'She was a queen who led the fight, / She fought for her country and her right. / With a shining sword in her hand, / Her son on her back in a cloth band / Remembered as a warrior grand, / She died defending her beloved Motherland. Who is she?'
Think of any other women warriors who made a significant contribution to India's struggle for Independence. Share your answers.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — Rani Abbakka. Match word meanings and complete the cause-and-effect table.

question_bank.stem: Match the words in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2: (1) vassal (n) — (i)–(xiv) options including extreme anger, take hold of suddenly, happen in a violent way, acting without thinking, sovereign country, association/partnership, wrath, seize, resolve, raged, decisive, apprehended, dauntless, avenge.
question_bank.table:
Word|Meaning
vassal (n)|a country/state that is controlled by a more powerful country and has to pay money when needed
coronation (n)|a ceremony at which a person is made king or queen
successor (n)|a person who succeeds another
rash (adj.)|acting or doing something without thinking about the consequences
sovereign country (n)|a country with a government having complete authority
league (n)|association or partnership
wrath (n)|extreme anger
seize (v)|take hold of suddenly or forcibly
resolve (n)|determination
raged (v)|happen in a violent way
decisive (adj.)|showing the ability to make decisions effectively
apprehended (v)|capture
dauntless (adj.)|fearless
avenge (v)|take revenge for

question_bank.stem: Complete the cause-and-effect table based on your understanding of the story: (1. Ullal had been captured by the Portuguese → (i) Ullal had to pay tribute to the colonial power. 2. → (ii) Rani Abbakka's husband Veera Narsimha felt it was a hasty decision. 3. Veera Narsimha ordered the soldiers to stop Rani Abbakka → (iii) ___. 4. Rani Abbakka addressed a meeting of rajas for their support, who felt they would be no match for the Portuguese → (iv) ___. 5. → (v) Rani Abbakka in turn attacked the Portuguese Fort.)
question_bank.table:
Cause|Effect
Ullal had been captured by the Portuguese|(i) Ullal had to pay a tribute to the colonial power.
(Cause 2)|(ii) Rani Abbakka's husband Veera Narsimha felt it was a hasty decision.
Verera Narsimha ordered the soldiers to stop Rani Abbakka when she said she would not be frightened by the Portuguese.|(iii)
Rani Abbakka addressed a meeting of rajas for their support, who felt they would be no match for the Portuguese army and their power.|(iv)
(Cause 5)|(v) Rani Abbakka in turn attacked the Portuguese Fort.

listening.transcript_text: TEACHER'S TALK (Rani Gaidinliu): Hello children, you all have read about Rani Abbakka. Let me share with you some details about one more braveheart. Her name is Rani Gaidinliu. Gaidinliu was born on 26 January 1915, in the present-day Tamenglong district of Manipur. She was a Naga leader who led an armed uprising against the British in Manipur, Nagaland, and Assam. Gaidinliu belonged to the Rongmei tribe, also known as the Kabui tribe. In 1927, at the age of 13, Gaidinliu joined the Heraka movement. This movement aimed at the revival of the Naga tribal religion and the establishment of self-rule for the Nagas, ending British rule. Gaidinliu was arrested in 1932 when she was just 16 and was sentenced to life imprisonment by the British rulers. Between 1932 and 1947, she was kept in several prisons across the Northeast. After being released in 1947, she continued to work for the betterment of her community. The then Prime Minister described Gaidinliu as the 'Daughter of the Hills' and gave her the title 'Rani' for her courage. She was also awarded the Padma Bhushan in 1982. In 1991, Rani Gaidinliu returned to her birthplace, where she died on 17 February 1993 at the age of 78. Rani Gaidinliu was conferred the Birsa Munda Award posthumously. The State Government has developed a park along with a statue to honour Rani Gaidinliu in Silchar, Assam. The Government of India issued a postal stamp in her honour in 1996 and a commemorative coin in 2015.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to the teacher speak about a braveheart. As you listen, complete the bio-table given below. (Bio-table fields: Full Name, Date of Birth, Place of Birth, Tribe, Age when joined Heraka Movement, Main aim of Movement, Year of Arrest, Age of Arrest, Sentence, Years of Imprisonment, Title Given, Award, Year of Death, Honour in Silchar, Postal Stamp Issued, Commemorative Coin Issued.)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Some of the words from the story 'Rani Abbakka' are given below. Work in pairs and pronounce these words by referring to the notes given. Emphasise the highlighted syllable. Remember: single syllable (monosyllabic) words do not have any stress. Words: vassal (va-sul, two syllables), successor (suhk·seh·suh, three syllables), rash (monosyllabic, /a/ like in rat), league (monosyllabic, long -ee), wrath (monosyllabic, -w is silent), seize (monosyllabic, not to be confused with 'size'), resolve (ri-zolv, two syllables, -s becomes /z/).

question_bank.stem: Pronounce the words and practise their stress: vassal (va-sul), successor (suhk·seh·suh), rash, league, wrath, seize, resolve (ri-zolv).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: India's past was heroic, and at present, the pace of her development is inspiring. Women play a significant role in it. Write a conversation between two students, Pratap and Tarana, discussing the role played by women in forming India's identity as a strong nation. Begin: 'Pratap: Tarana, I have a lot of respect for women like... / Tarana: I believe in the capabilities of women. Did you...?' Complete the conversation in about six to eight sentences for each of them. Share your answers with classmates and the teacher.

Study the spelling of 'seize', 'sovereign', 'their', 'soldiers' — these words have 'ei' and 'ie'. Spelling tip: 'i' before 'e' except after 'c'. Fill in the blanks with 'ie' or 'ei' for the following words.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with 'ie' or 'ei': (1) c___ling (2) bel___ve (3) th___f (4) ach___ve (5) perc___ve (6) prot___n (7) soc___ty (8) sh___ld (9) w___ght (10) fr___nd

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the word 'wrath' — the initial 'w' is silent. There are many words in English where certain letters are not pronounced but are spelt (silent letters). For example: campaign, knowledge, pneumonia, honour, calm. Find more words with silent letters.

question_bank.stem: Find more words with silent letters (beyond campaign, knowledge, pneumonia, honour, calm). Share your findings with your classmates and the teacher.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the expressions from the text: 'laughing stock', 'last laugh'. Use the dictionary page given to find the meanings of: burst out laughing, don't make me laugh, laugh until you cry, have a good laugh. Now use these expressions in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: Find the meanings of these expressions (using the dictionary page): burst out laughing, don't make me laugh, laugh until you cry, have a good laugh. Then use each expression in a sentence of your own.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study direct and indirect speech conversion rules. Direct speech uses the speaker's exact words. In indirect (reported) speech, changes are made to tense (back-shift), pronouns, and time/place expressions. Rewrite the following sentences from direct speech to indirect speech.

question_bank.stem: Rewrite in indirect speech: (1) The teacher said, 'Women of valour have made significant contributions throughout history.' → The teacher remarked that ___. (2) 'I enjoy reading historical novels,' said Smitha. → Smitha shared that ___. (3) The captain said to the coach, 'Our team won the championship last year.' → The captain told the coach that ___. (4) The class teacher said, 'Please turn off the lights when you leave.' → The class teacher requested ___. (5) 'Finish the work before dinner,' said mother to Ravi. → Mother advised ___. (6) The vet said to the pet owner, 'Take the dog for a walk.' → The vet instructed ___.

Complete the paragraph by transforming the dialogue (Captain: 'We need to focus on our strategy for the final match.' / Player: 'I have some ideas on how we can improve our strategy.' / Captain: 'Make sure to share those ideas with the team during practice.') into indirect speech: 'The Captain remarked that 1. ________. To which one of the players replied that 2. ________. The Captain advised the player to 3. ________.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In groups of five, find facts about any one brave woman who we remember because of her achievements and present in class. For example: Rani Chennamma of Kittur, Rani Durgavati, Ahilyabai Holkar, Jhalkari Bai, Captain Lakshmi Sahgal, Kanaklata Barua, Kalpana Chawla, or anyone else. Include in your presentation: (1) Childhood; (2) Training/Education; (3) Achievements; (4) Contributions; (5) What you have learnt from her.

question_bank.stem: In groups of five, research one brave woman (e.g. Rani Chennamma, Rani Durgavati, Ahilyabai Holkar, Jhalkari Bai, Captain Lakshmi Sahgal, Kanaklata Barua, Kalpana Chawla, or another) and present in class. Cover: Childhood, Training/Education, Achievements, Contributions, and What you have learnt from her.
